name: doit (Python Task Automation Tool)

performance.notes: Fast up-to-date checking via file hash or timestamp; skips unchanged tasks

agent_readiness.notes: Python-based make replacement. Define tasks in dodo.py. def task_compile(): return {'actions': ['gcc -o hello hello.c'], 'targets': ['hello'], 'file_dep': ['hello.c']}. def task_test(): return {'actions': ['pytest'], 'task_dep': ['compile']}. doit (CLI). Up-to-date check: file hash or mtime. Python callables as actions: 'actions': [my_function]. Generators for dynamic task lists. doit list — list tasks. doit run task_name. Subtasks: for i in range(3): yield {'name': str(i), ...}. doit.api for programmatic use. Reporter: console, json. Parallel: doit -n 4.

gotchas: dodo.py must be in current directory or specified explicitly
No built-in support for virtual environments — must configure manually
Parallel execution requires tasks to be independent — no automatic dep check
No type annotations — limited IDE support
Less known than Make/invoke — steeper learning curve for newcomers
best_when: Python-centric build automation where Make is unwieldy
Data science pipelines with file-based incrementality
Projects needing Python callables as build actions
Replacing Makefile with pure Python build logic
avoid_when: Cross-language builds (C/C++ projects — use CMake or Make)
Large orchestration workflows (use Prefect or Dagster)
Teams unfamiliar with Python (use Make or Just)
summary: doit is a Python task automation tool (make replacement). Define tasks in dodo.py with actions, targets, file_dep, task_dep. Incremental execution via file hash. Python callables as actions. CLI: doit / doit task_name / doit -n 4 for parallel. MIT by Eduardo Schettino. Best for Python-centric build automation with incremental file checking. Simpler than Make for Python projects. Less known than invoke. No built-in venv management.
